prep.title: The Starting Line
prep.subtitle: Preparing together, building healthy habits, aligning on parenting values.
prep.keyDevDesc: Creating the best environment for conception.
prep.dadPriorityDesc: Start taking multivitamins and limit alcohol.
babyDevBullets: No baby yet! This is the preparation phase.
Sperm takes about 74 days to mature, so your health now affects conception 2.5 months from now.
Your partner's body needs 400-800mcg of folic acid daily to prevent neural tube defects once conception occurs.

symptomTable.name: Temperature Tracking
symptomTable.help: Help her remember to take temp first thing in morning

emotionalScripts: What kind of parents do we want to be?
Are there any fears we should talk about before we start trying?
practicalTasks: Start taking a men's multivitamin with zinc and selenium.
Cut back on alcohol, quit smoking.
Review your health insurance maternity coverage.
relationshipIdeas: Have a 'pre-TTC' (Trying To Conceive) special date night to celebrate this new chapter.

bondingTasks.title: Values List
bondingTasks.text: Write down 3 values you want to instill in your future child.

medicalRoadmap: Schedule a pre-conception checkup for your partner.
Get dental checkups (gum health affects pregnancy).
questions: Are all our vaccinations up to date?
takeaways: Pregnancy health starts months before conception.
Your habits matter just as much as hers right now.

1.title: The First Week
1.subtitle: Technically, you're not pregnant yet.
1.keyDevDesc: Uterus preparing for a new egg.
babyDevBullets: Week 1 of pregnancy actually begins on the first day of her last menstrual period (LMP).
There is no baby yet. The body is shedding the old uterine lining to prepare a fresh bed for a fertilized egg.

symptomTable.name: Cramping
symptomTable.help: Offer a heating pad and ibuprofen

emotionalScripts: Do you need anything to feel more comfortable today?
practicalTasks: Keep track of the exact start date of this period—it will be used to calculate the due date later!
Stock up on her preferred period products and comfort foods.
relationshipIdeas: Low-key movie night with a heating pad.

bondingTasks.title: Mark the Calendar
bondingTasks.text: Record the LMP date in a shared calendar.

takeaways: Due dates are calculated from this week.
Record the date—it's very important.

2.title: The Ovulation Window
2.subtitle: The egg is released and waiting.
2.keyDevDesc: The egg travels down the fallopian tube.
2.partnerSymptomsDesc: Mild cramping (mittelschmerz), changes in cervical mucus.
babyDevBullets: Ovulation usually occurs around day 14 of a 28-day cycle.
An egg is released from the ovary and travels into the fallopian tube, where it waits for sperm.
Sperm can live inside the reproductive tract for up to 5 days, but the egg only survives for 12-24 hours.
emotionalScripts: Let's keep this fun and not just a clinical chore.
practicalTasks: Be available and responsive to the ovulation window.
Avoid using hot tubs or saunas (heat kills sperm).
relationshipIdeas: Focus on romance and intimacy without pressure.
medicalRoadmap: Have intercourse during the fertile window (the days leading up to and day of ovulation).
takeaways: Timing is crucial for conception.
Keep intimacy stress-free.

3.title: Fertilization & Implantation
3.subtitle: The spark of life happens.
3.dadPriorityDesc: Wait before taking a pregnancy test.
babyDevBullets: Sperm meets egg, creating a single cell called a zygote.
The zygote divides rapidly into a ball of cells (blastocyst) as it travels to the uterus.
Implantation begins: the blastocyst burrows into the nutrient-rich uterine lining.

symptomTable.name: Implantation Bleeding
symptomTable.help: Reassure her it's normal and harmless

emotionalScripts: I know the two-week wait is stressful. How are you holding up?
practicalTasks: Distract her from symptom-spotting.
Do NOT encourage taking a pregnancy test yet—it's too early for accurate results and can cause unnecessary heartbreak.
relationshipIdeas: Plan a fun, engaging outing that takes your minds off waiting.
medicalRoadmap: Avoid alcohol and medications not approved for pregnancy just in case.
takeaways: Implantation is happening now.
It is still too early to test.

4.title: Tiny Heartbeat Begins
4.subtitle: Most couples confirm pregnancy this week.
4.keyDevDesc: Neural tube closes, heart starts pulsing.
4.dadPriorityDesc: Take the test, book the OB, support her.
babyDevBullets: Implantation completes, triggering the production of hCG (detected by tests).
Neural tube closes: the foundation of the brain and spinal cord takes shape.
The heart tube forms and begins pulsing at 80-85 beats per minute.
emotionalScripts: I am here with you, whatever the result is.
We are going to figure this out together.
practicalTasks: Buy a 2-pack of digital pregnancy tests.
Call to schedule the first OB-GYN appointment.
Start a pregnancy folder for documents.
relationshipIdeas: Celebrate the positive test privately just the two of you.

bondingTasks.title: Take a Photo
bondingTasks.text: Take a picture of her holding the positive test (keep it private for now).

medicalRoadmap: Call OB to schedule an 8-week dating scan.
questions: What is our estimated due date based on LMP?
takeaways: You are officially going to be a dad.
Book the doctor appointment ASAP.

5.title: Morning Sickness Peaks
5.subtitle: The reality of pregnancy physical symptoms hits.
5.keyDevDesc: Brain, spinal cord, blood vessels develop.
5.partnerSymptomsDesc: All-day sickness, food aversions, sore breasts.
babyDevBullets: The embryo resembles a tiny tadpole.
The neural tube is developing rapidly into the brain.
Arm and leg buds begin to appear.
The heart beats steadily and circulates blood.

symptomTable.name: Nausea
symptomTable.help: Stock crackers, avoid cooking smelly foods

symptomTable.name: Frequent Urination
symptomTable.help: Ensure path to bathroom is clear at night

emotionalScripts: You rest. I've got dinner and the chores tonight.
practicalTasks: Take over cooking permanently for the first trimester.
Remove the litter box duty entirely (toxoplasmosis risk).
Keep a water bottle filled for her at all times.
relationshipIdeas: Eat your meals in another room if the smell of your food makes her nauseous.
medicalRoadmap: Write down questions for your upcoming OB appointment.
takeaways: Morning sickness can happen 24/7.
Your main job is keeping her fed and rested.

6.title: The First Ultrasound
6.subtitle: Seeing the tiny flicker of life.
babyDevBullets: The heartbeat can often be seen via transvaginal ultrasound.
Facial features like eyes and ears are forming.
The embryo's body is starting to curve into a C-shape.

symptomTable.name: Extreme Fatigue
symptomTable.help: Let her sleep as much as she needs

emotionalScripts: It was amazing seeing that heartbeat with you.
practicalTasks: Take time off work to attend the first ultrasound.
Keep the house quiet so she can nap.
Handle the grocery shopping alone.
relationshipIdeas: Look at the ultrasound printout together and marvel at it.

bondingTasks.title: Ultrasound Frame
bondingTasks.text: Put the first ultrasound picture on the fridge.

medicalRoadmap: First OB visit: expect bloodwork, urine test, and ultrasound.
takeaways: Seeing the heartbeat drops miscarriage risk significantly.
Her fatigue is a medical reality, not laziness.

7.title: Brain Developing Rapidly
7.subtitle: A million new brain cells every minute.
7.dadPriorityDesc: Sneak protein into whatever she can stomach.
babyDevBullets: The brain is growing faster than the rest of the body.
Kidneys are forming and preparing to produce urine.
Hands and feet look like tiny paddles.

symptomTable.name: Food Aversions
symptomTable.help: Don't force healthy foods; feed her what she craves

emotionalScripts: If you only want to eat toast today, I will make you the best toast.
practicalTasks: Find foods she can tolerate (bland carbs are usually safe).
Clean the fridge of anything that triggers her nausea.
relationshipIdeas: Give her a gentle foot massage.

bondingTasks.title: First Words
bondingTasks.text: Start talking to her belly. The baby can't hear yet, but it builds the habit.

medicalRoadmap: Discuss prenatal testing options (NIPT, NT scan) with your doctor.
questions: When do we schedule genetic screening?
takeaways: Survival mode for food is okay right now.
The brain is developing at an astonishing rate.

8.title: Fingers and Toes
8.subtitle: Losing the tadpole tail.
8.keyDevDesc: Fingers and toes start to differentiate.
8.partnerSymptomsDesc: Pants feeling tight due to progesterone.
babyDevBullets: The embryonic tail disappears.
Nerve cells are branching out to connect with one another.
Breathing tubes extend from throat to developing lungs.

symptomTable.name: Bloating
symptomTable.help: Compliment her body, buy comfortable loungewear

emotionalScripts: You look beautiful. Your body is doing incredible work.
practicalTasks: Draft a preliminary baby budget.
Research life insurance policies if you don't have them.
relationshipIdeas: Order her favorite takeout and watch a comedy.

bondingTasks.title: Money Talk
bondingTasks.text: Have a calm, 15-minute alignment chat on baby finances.

mustBuy: Stretchy sweatpants or leggings for her
medicalRoadmap: If you haven't had the dating ultrasound yet, it will likely happen this week.
takeaways: The embryo is starting to look distinctly human.
It's time to get your finances organized.

9.title: All Systems Go
9.subtitle: All essential organs have formed.
9.keyDevDesc: Major organs are in place and begin functioning.
9.dadPriorityDesc: First trimester drag is real. Keep spirits up.
babyDevBullets: The critical period of organ development is nearly complete.
Heart finishes dividing into four chambers.
Tiny teeth are forming under the gums.

symptomTable.name: Mood Swings
symptomTable.help: Be an unshakeable rock. Don't take it personally.

emotionalScripts: It's okay to feel overwhelmed. I've got everything handled.
practicalTasks: Take over laundry and heavy lifting entirely.
Make sure she is drinking 8-10 glasses of water daily.
relationshipIdeas: Leave a supportive sticky note on her mirror.

bondingTasks.title: Name Game
bondingTasks.text: Start throwing out wild baby names just to make her laugh.

medicalRoadmap: Schedule the NIPT (Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing) blood draw for week 10 if opting in.
questions: Do we want to know the gender early?
takeaways: The riskiest developmental phase is almost over.
Keep her hydrated and handle the house.

10.title: Officially a Fetus
10.subtitle: Graduating from embryo to fetus.
10.keyDevDesc: Bones begin to harden, joints can bend.
10.dadPriorityDesc: Support her through NIPT blood draws.
10.partnerSymptomsDesc: Increased blood volume makes veins visible.
babyDevBullets: Officially called a fetus now!
Kidneys are producing urine.
Webbed fingers and toes have completely separated.
emotionalScripts: We are one step closer to the safe zone.
practicalTasks: Drive her to the lab for the NIPT blood test.
Plan how/when you want to announce the pregnancy.
relationshipIdeas: Discuss who you want to tell first and how.

bondingTasks.title: The Secret
bondingTasks.text: Enjoy knowing a huge secret that the rest of the world doesn't.

medicalRoadmap: NIPT blood draw (screens for chromosomal abnormalities and can reveal gender).
questions: How long does NIPT results take?
takeaways: The embryo is now a fetus.
Blood volume is rising rapidly, causing headaches and visible veins.
